takeaway: The Interaction Design Foundation lays out Garrett's five planes in order — strategy, scope, structure, skeleton, surface — and frames strategy as the load-bearing base: of the strategy plane it says "Everything else rests on this part," with each higher plane growing more concrete.

The UX Design Institute frames the model as a progression from abstract to concrete: "The bottom plane of the model is Strategy," where objectives for clients and users are set, rising to a surface plane focused on "the users' sensory experience" — color, texture, and visual presentation.

User experience (UX) design is a user-centered, research-driven discipline covering all aspects of how people interact with a product, not aesthetics alone; it notes "Information architecture is the art and science of structuring and organizing the information in products and services to support usability and findability."

The Nielsen Norman Group's canonical definition is deliberately broad: "'User experience' encompasses all aspects of the end-user's interaction with the company, its services, and its products" — far more than the interface or usability alone.

Usability measures how effectively and satisfyingly users accomplish tasks, and impatience is the default: as Jakob Nielsen puts it, "People don't want to wait. And they don't want to learn how to use a home page."

User-centered design — the spirit of Garrett's strategy plane — builds around real user needs so the product adapts to the user, and is "based on the understanding of a user, their demands, priorities and experiences," which "can lead to increased product usefulness and usability."

The scope plane turns strategy into concrete features, and a functional requirement is the unit of that work: it "defines a function of a system or its component, where a function is described as a summary (or specification or statement) of behavior between inputs and outputs."

The scope plane covers content as well as features; content strategy is the discipline of "getting the right content to the right user at the right time through strategic planning" — planning what words and media the product needs before anyone arranges them.

The structure plane is largely information architecture (IA) — "the structural design of shared information environments" — organizing content so it is findable and the system makes sense as the user moves through it.

The structure plane also fixes interaction design — how the product behaves in response to the user; interaction designers aim to make products that "respond to the user's experience and not the other way around."

The skeleton plane is where layout gets specific, and the wireframe is its tool — a structural blueprint of content, navigation, and interface elements placed before visual design, the point where "thinking becomes tangible."

Skeleton-plane work is often prototyped before commitment; a prototype lets "users of the software to evaluate developers' proposals for the design of the eventual product by actually trying them out, rather than having to interpret and evaluate the design based on descriptions."

Good structure and skeleton lean on affordances — perceivable cues for what is possible; as Don Norman frames it, "Affordances determine what actions are possible. Signifiers communicate where the action should take place," which is how an interface tells users what they can do.

The surface plane is the visible result, and web history shows the tension it manages: early on "design and good aesthetics seemed to take precedence over good markup structure" — a reminder that surface without the planes beneath it is decoration.

Surface-plane choices are graphic-design choices — typography, color, imagery — and they carry meaning: "Typography can influence how information is perceived and remembered," so the sensory layer shapes trust and comprehension, not just looks.

Why the lower planes matter is captured by the two gulfs — figuring out how to act (execution) and understanding what happened (evaluation); "without effective design elements to support users, they can become insurmountable barriers between users and their goals."
